How do I process audio in real-time with double buffering?
How do I compute a spectrogram for frequency analysis?

Spectrogram = short-time FFT (STFT):
```python
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import spectrogram
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Parameters
fs = 48000  # sample rate
nperseg = 1024  # FFT window size
noverlap = 512  # 50% overlap

f, t, Sxx = spectrogram(signal, fs, nperseg=nperseg, noverlap=noverlap,
                         window='hann', scaling='density')

plt.pcolormesh(t, f, 10*np.log10(Sxx), shading='gouraud', vmin=-80)
plt.ylabel('Frequency [Hz]')
plt.xlabel('Time [s]')
plt.colorbar(label='PSD [dB/Hz]')
```
For embedded: compute overlapping FFT blocks, store magnitude. Trade-offs:
- Longer window → better frequency resolution, worse time resolution
- 50% overlap is standard, 75% for better visualization
- Hanning window reduces spectral leakage

What is the frequency resolution of a 64-point FFT at 8000Hz sample rate?

Frequency resolution = fs / N = 8000 / 64 = 125.00 Hz per bin.

Total bins: 33 (DC to Nyquist)
Nyquist frequency: 4000 Hz
Frame duration: 8.0 ms

Short frame — good time resolution but poor frequency resolution. Consider overlap-add.

Memory: 512 bytes (complex float32). Processing time on Cortex-M4F @ 168MHz: ~0.02ms.

Nyquist frequency: 8000 Hz

Memory: 512 bytes (complex float32). Processing time on Cortex-M4F @ 168MHz: ~0.02ms.

Should I use a Hanning window for vibration analysis FFT analysis?

Hanning window for vibration analysis:

Good general-purpose. Main lobe width: 4 bins. Side lobe: -31dB. Use for narrowband signal detection.

Vibration analysis: Hanning is the standard choice for vibration — good balance of resolution and leakage. Use with overlap processing.
Should I use a Hanning window for audio spectrum analyzer FFT analysis?

Hanning window for audio spectrum analyzer:

Good general-purpose. Main lobe width: 4 bins. Side lobe: -31dB. Use for narrowband signal detection.

Audio spectrum: Standard choice for audio analyzers. 50% overlap with Hanning gives COLA (Constant Overlap-Add) property.
Should I use a Hanning window for radar signal processing FFT analysis?

Hanning window for radar signal processing:

Good general-purpose. Main lobe width: 4 bins. Side lobe: -31dB. Use for narrowband signal detection.

Radar: Common in radar signal processing. Good for moving target detection.
Should I use a Hanning window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG) FFT analysis?

Hanning window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG):

Good general-purpose. Main lobe width: 4 bins. Side lobe: -31dB. Use for narrowband signal detection.

Biomedical: Standard for EEG/ECG spectral analysis. Use with 50% overlap.
Should I use a Hamming window for vibration analysis FFT analysis?

Hamming window for vibration analysis:

Similar to Hanning but higher side lobes (-43dB first side lobe). Better frequency resolution, worse leakage rejection.

Vibration analysis: Acceptable alternative. Side lobe behavior slightly different from Hanning.
Should I use a Hamming window for audio spectrum analyzer FFT analysis?

Hamming window for audio spectrum analyzer:

Similar to Hanning but higher side lobes (-43dB first side lobe). Better frequency resolution, worse leakage rejection.

Audio spectrum: Usable but Hanning preferred for audio due to COLA property.
Should I use a Hamming window for radar signal processing FFT analysis?

Hamming window for radar signal processing:

Similar to Hanning but higher side lobes (-43dB first side lobe). Better frequency resolution, worse leakage rejection.

Radar: Used in some radar systems for its first side lobe characteristics.
Should I use a Hamming window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG) FFT analysis?

Hamming window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG):

Similar to Hanning but higher side lobes (-43dB first side lobe). Better frequency resolution, worse leakage rejection.

Biomedical: Acceptable for biomedical. Similar performance to Hanning for EEG bands.
Should I use a Blackman window for vibration analysis FFT analysis?

Blackman window for vibration analysis:

Excellent side lobe rejection (-58dB). Wider main lobe (6 bins). Use for signals with large dynamic range.

Vibration analysis: Good for detecting small vibration components near large ones.
Should I use a Blackman window for audio spectrum analyzer FFT analysis?

Blackman window for audio spectrum analyzer:

Excellent side lobe rejection (-58dB). Wider main lobe (6 bins). Use for signals with large dynamic range.

Audio spectrum: Good for audio with wide dynamic range (music mastering, noise floor analysis).
Should I use a Blackman window for radar signal processing FFT analysis?

Blackman window for radar signal processing:

Excellent side lobe rejection (-58dB). Wider main lobe (6 bins). Use for signals with large dynamic range.

Radar: Good for radar with close targets at different power levels.
Should I use a Blackman window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG) FFT analysis?

Blackman window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG):

Excellent side lobe rejection (-58dB). Wider main lobe (6 bins). Use for signals with large dynamic range.

Biomedical: Good for detecting low-amplitude EEG rhythms near high-amplitude ones.
Should I use a Flat-top window for vibration analysis FFT analysis?

Flat-top window for vibration analysis:

Best amplitude accuracy (+-0.01dB). Very wide main lobe. Use for calibration and amplitude measurement.

Vibration analysis: Use when precise vibration amplitude matters more than frequency resolution.
Should I use a Flat-top window for audio spectrum analyzer FFT analysis?

Flat-top window for audio spectrum analyzer:

Best amplitude accuracy (+-0.01dB). Very wide main lobe. Use for calibration and amplitude measurement.

Audio spectrum: Not typical for audio display — main lobe too wide for frequency discrimination.
Should I use a Flat-top window for radar signal processing FFT analysis?

Flat-top window for radar signal processing:

Best amplitude accuracy (+-0.01dB). Very wide main lobe. Use for calibration and amplitude measurement.

Radar: Not typical in radar — frequency resolution more important than amplitude accuracy.
Should I use a Flat-top window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG) FFT analysis?

Flat-top window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG):

Best amplitude accuracy (+-0.01dB). Very wide main lobe. Use for calibration and amplitude measurement.

Biomedical: Use when precise power measurement in specific EEG bands matters.
Should I use a Kaiser window for vibration analysis FFT analysis?

Kaiser window for vibration analysis:

Adjustable trade-off via beta parameter. beta=0 (rectangular), beta=5 (similar to Hamming), beta=10 (similar to Blackman).

Vibration analysis: Kaiser with beta=6-8 is common. Flexible choice.
Should I use a Kaiser window for audio spectrum analyzer FFT analysis?

Kaiser window for audio spectrum analyzer:

Adjustable trade-off via beta parameter. beta=0 (rectangular), beta=5 (similar to Hamming), beta=10 (similar to Blackman).

Audio spectrum: Good flexibility. Use beta=5-7 for audio applications.
Should I use a Kaiser window for radar signal processing FFT analysis?

Kaiser window for radar signal processing:

Adjustable trade-off via beta parameter. beta=0 (rectangular), beta=5 (similar to Hamming), beta=10 (similar to Blackman).

Radar: Preferred in radar — can optimize for specific side lobe requirements. Dolph-Chebyshev (related) also popular.
Should I use a Kaiser window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG) FFT analysis?

Kaiser window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG):

Adjustable trade-off via beta parameter. beta=0 (rectangular), beta=5 (similar to Hamming), beta=10 (similar to Blackman).

Biomedical: Good choice — beta can be tuned for specific clinical requirements.
Should I use a Rectangular (no window) window for vibration analysis FFT analysis?

Rectangular (no window) window for vibration analysis:

Narrowest main lobe (2 bins) but worst leakage (-13dB side lobes). Use only for transient analysis or when signal is exactly periodic in the frame.

Vibration analysis: Avoid for vibration — spectral leakage obscures harmonics.
Should I use a Rectangular (no window) window for audio spectrum analyzer FFT analysis?

Rectangular (no window) window for audio spectrum analyzer:

Narrowest main lobe (2 bins) but worst leakage (-13dB side lobes). Use only for transient analysis or when signal is exactly periodic in the frame.

Audio spectrum: Not recommended — severe leakage makes spectrum hard to interpret.
Should I use a Rectangular (no window) window for radar signal processing FFT analysis?

Rectangular (no window) window for radar signal processing:

Narrowest main lobe (2 bins) but worst leakage (-13dB side lobes). Use only for transient analysis or when signal is exactly periodic in the frame.

Radar: Sometimes used in pulsed radar for maximum frequency resolution.
Should I use a Rectangular (no window) window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG) FFT analysis?

Rectangular (no window) window for biomedical signal (ECG/EEG):

Narrowest main lobe (2 bins) but worst leakage (-13dB side lobes). Use only for transient analysis or when signal is exactly periodic in the frame.

Biomedical: Avoid for clinical analysis — spectral leakage can mask important features.
